name: Periyavaccan Pillai
dates_notes: Senior contemporary of Pillai-Lokācārya; sectarian chronicles plus manuscript colophons. Honored throughout the Tenkalai tradition as 'Vyākhyā-Cakravartī' — emperor among commentators — for the comprehensiveness of his Divya-Prabandham commentaries.
core_thesis:
Periyavāccān Piḷḷai is the *vyākhyā-cakravarti* — the "emperor among commentators" — of the Tenkalai tradition, the figure through whom the Āḻvārs' *Divya-Prabandham* receives comprehensive Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa exposition for the first time. The Viśiṣṭādvaita doctrinal architecture is preserved without revision: Brahman as Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī, qualified by *cit* and *acit* in *apṛthak-siddha* relation as his body (*Tiruvāymoḻi-Vyākhyāna*, on *Tiruvāymoḻi* 1.1.1, on *uyarvu-uyar nalam uṭaiyavan*); the *jīva* as eternal *aṃśa* with *jñātṛtva* and *kartṛtva* dependent on Bhagavān's *anumati* (*Tiruvāymoḻi-Vyākhyāna*, on *Tiruvāymoḻi* 7.1.1 ff.); the *prabandham* as *Drāviḍa-Veda*, *śruti*-equivalent in interpretive force (*Vyākhyāna-Avatārikā* on *Tiruvāymoḻi*).

The contribution that defines his place in the lineage is the comprehensiveness of the *vyākhyāna*-corpus. Periyavāccān is the only Śrīvaiṣṇava commentator to produce a complete Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on the entire *Nālāyira-Divya-Prabandham* — the four-thousand verse Āḻvār corpus including the *Tiruvāymoḻi*, the *Tiruviruttam*, the *Tiruvāciriyam*, the *Periya-Tiruvantāti*, the *Tiruppāvai*, the *Periyāḻvār Tirumoḻi*, the *Tiruppallāṇṭu*, and the remaining *prabandhas*. The standard commentaries on the *Tiruppāvai* (*Periyavāccān-Piḷḷai-Vyākhyāna* on Āṇṭāḷ's 30 verses), the *Tiruppallāṇṭu* (his *vyākhyāna* fixes the school's reading of Periyāḻvār's *maṅgala*-hymn), and the *Periya-Tirumoḻi* (his *vyākhyāna* on Tirumaṅgai-Āḻvār's verses) become the lineage's settled readings.

The commentaries are doctrinal in form: each Āḻvār-verse is opened first philologically, then doctrinally, with explicit cross-reference to the school's settled positions on *prapatti*, *bhāgavata-śeṣatva*, and *anugraha*. The work establishes the practice of reading the *Divya-Prabandham* as a doctrinal corpus in its own right — *Drāviḍa-Veda* with the same interpretive authority as the Sanskrit Upaniṣads — and is the textual base on which Pillai-Lokācārya's school-defining articulation of *prapatti*-soteriology and Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ's later exposition both rest. Without Periyavāccān's *vyākhyāna*-corpus the *prabandham*-reading practice that defines the Tenkalai school would lack its commentarial foundation.

engaged_works.title: Tiruvaymoli-Vyakhyana (Twenty-Four-Thousand-padi)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Nāmmāḻvār's Tiruvāymoḻi, transmitted in the Śrīvaiṣṇava manuscript canon and recited in the school's daily kālakṣepam tradition.
engaged_works.summary: The *Twenty-Four-Thousand-paṭi* is Periyavāccān's Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Nāmmāḻvār's *Tiruvāymoḻi* — the third of the four canonical *Tiruvāymoḻi*-commentaries (after Tirukkurukai-Pirān Piḷḷān's 6000-paṭi and Naṅjīyar's 9000-paṭi, and before Vaḍakkutiruvīdhi-Piḷḷai's 36000-paṭi), and the longest commentary on the *Tiruvāymoḻi* in the Śrīvaiṣṇava canon excepting only the *Īḍu*. The method is verse-by-verse exposition: each of the 1102 verses is opened first philologically (Tamil grammar, lexical glosses, syntactic resolution), then doctrinally (cross-reference to the school's settled positions on Brahman-as-Nārāyaṇa, on *prapatti*, on *bhāgavata-śeṣatva*), then devotionally (the *bhāva*-mode in which the Āḻvār is speaking). Particularly load-bearing are the openings: *Tiruvāymoḻi* 1.1.1 (*uyarvu-uyar nalam uṭaiyavan*) is read as the verse's own statement that Bhagavān's *kalyāṇa-guṇas* are infinite and not exhaustively enumerable, fixing the school's *para-tva* doctrine in Tamil. *Tiruvāymoḻi* 6.10.10 — the *carama-prapatti*-verse — is read as the Āḻvār's enactment of *śaraṇāgita* in the form Pillai-Lokācārya will later articulate scholastically. *Tiruvāymoḻi* 10.10.11 — the closing *phala-śruti* — is read as fixing the *bhāgavata-niṣṭhā* that Pillai-Lokācārya's [*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§406–438](cite://pillai-lokacarya/shri-vacana-bhushana/406-438) will articulate as *bhāgavata-śeṣatva*. The commentary is the school's standard mid-length *Tiruvāymoḻi*-reading and is read in *kālakṣepam* (oral exposition) across the Tenkalai *maṭhas*.

engaged_works.title: Tiruppavai-Vyakhyana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Āṇṭāḷ's Tiruppāvai.
engaged_works.summary: Periyavāccān's commentary on Āṇṭāḷ's *Tiruppāvai* — the 30-verse Āḻvār-poem of the *mārgaḻi*-month *vrata* in which Āṇṭāḷ and her companion *gopīs* awaken Kṛṣṇa to receive their petition for *kaiṅkarya* (eternal service). The commentary establishes the school's settled reading of the *Tiruppāvai* and is the principal Śrīvaiṣṇava *vyākhyāna* on Āṇṭāḷ's poem. Three loci do the load-bearing doctrinal work. (i) The opening verse (*mārgaḻi tiṅkaḷ*) is read as Āṇṭāḷ's enactment of the *adhikārī*'s entry into *prapatti* through the *bhāgavata-saṃsarga* of the *gopīs*' company, fixing the school's reading of *prapatti* as community-mediated rather than as an individual act of cognition. (ii) Verses 24–28 (the *Govinda*-and-*Mādhava*-petitions) are read as the *prapanna*'s articulation of the *upāya*-and-*upeya* relation: Bhagavān is both means and end, and the petition asks for *kaiṅkarya* itself rather than for any subordinate *phala*. (iii) The closing verse (*vaṅkak-kaṭal*) is read as Āṇṭāḷ's self-identification of her *adhikāra* — she is Periyāḻvār's daughter, *bhāgavatī* by birth, and her *prapatti* is mediated through the *bhāgavata-paramparā*. The commentary is the standard Śrīvaiṣṇava reading of the *Tiruppāvai* and is recited in the school's daily *mārgaḻi*-month liturgy across the Śrīvaiṣṇava temples.

engaged_works.title: Stotra-Ratna-Vyakhyana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Yāmuna's Stotra-Ratna.
engaged_works.summary: Periyavāccān's Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentary on Yāmuna's *Stotra-Ratna* — the 65-verse Sanskrit hymn that fixes in compressed lyric form the doctrinal architecture of pre-Rāmānuja Śrīvaiṣṇavism. The commentary opens Yāmuna's compressed verses for the school's reading, with particular attention to the verses Pillai-Lokācārya later treats as foundational for Tenkalai *prapatti*-doctrine. *Stotra-Ratna* v. 22 (*na dharma-niṣṭho 'smi*) is the locus classicus of the *ākiñcana*-stance — the surrendered self's recognition of his own incapacity, on which Pillai-Lokācārya's [*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §83](cite://pillai-lokacarya/shri-vacana-bhushana/83) will rest the entire *prapatti*-soteriology. Periyavāccān's commentary fixes the reading of the verse as a positive *adhikāra*-statement (incapacity is the *adhikāra* for *prapatti*, not merely an empirical condition), in the form Pillai-Lokācārya's articulation will presuppose. *Stotra-Ratna* v. 60 (the *Catuḥ-Ślokī*-anticipation) is read as Yāmuna's first articulation of Śrī's *upāya*-status (later developed in his *Catuḥ-Ślokī* and fixed by Pillai-Lokācārya in [*Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* §§9–42](cite://pillai-lokacarya/shri-vacana-bhushana/9-42)). The commentary is the school's standard reading of Yāmuna's hymn and is read in the Tenkalai *kālakṣepam* tradition as the immediate precursor to Pillai-Lokācārya's rahasya-corpus.
